title: How to Get Marriage Certificate Online — Registration & Application Guide 2026
meta_description: Marriage certificate registration online 2026 — register marriage, documents needed, Hindu/Muslim/Christian/Special Marriage Act process, state-wise portals, fees ₹100-500.

hero.one_liner: Register your marriage and get a marriage certificate online — required for joint bank accounts, spouse visa, insurance nomination, and property inheritance rights

sections.heading: Why You Need a Marriage Certificate
content: A marriage certificate is the legal document proving that two people are married. While your marriage ceremony may be religious (temple, mosque, church) or civil, the CERTIFICATE is issued by the government and is the only legal proof accepted by courts, government agencies, and financial institutions.
Without it, proving marital status is extremely difficult.
Legal proof of marriage: It's the only legal document proving you are married. Courts, government agencies, and institutions accept only this.
No religious certificate or private record is legally valid. During inheritance, succession, pension claims, or divorce proceedings, a marriage certificate is essential.

Spouse visa and passport: Adding your spouse's name to your passport, applying for spouse visa to any country (US, UK, Canada, Germany, etc.), or renewing married passport all require marriage certificate. Embassies and immigration authorities will not accept any other document.
Even establishing 'common law marriage' status abroad requires a certified marriage certificate from India.

Joint bank accounts: Opening joint accounts with your spouse, adding spouse as nominee, or claiming joint property ownership all require marriage certificate. Banks require this for KYC verification and to ensure both parties consent.

Insurance and nominee benefits: Insurance policies, life insurance, health insurance — all allow you to nominate your spouse and claim spousal benefits only with marriage certificate. If you die without a certificate, your spouse may struggle to claim your life insurance, pension, or PF benefits.

Property registration and inheritance: Buying or registering property in joint names requires marriage certificate. Inheritance rights, succession, claiming spouse's pension after death, and will registration all require marriage certificate.
Government schemes for married couples: PM Awas Yojana (housing scheme), maternity benefits, spousal pension, family health insurance — many government schemes require proof of marriage for couples.
Divorce and legal separation: If you ever need divorce or legal separation, the marriage certificate is the primary document. Without it, courts cannot grant divorce and you'll face delays.
Children's documents: Registering your child's birth, updating birth certificate with father's name, or including spouse in child's guardianship requires marriage certificate.

sections.heading: Is Marriage Registration Mandatory in India?
content: Technically yes. The Supreme Court (in Seema vs.
Ashwani Kumar case, 2006) made marriage registration compulsory in India. However, enforcement varies widely across states.

While your marriage is legally valid if performed under religious customs (Hindu, Muslim, Christian, etc.) without registration, having a certificate is practically essential for all government and legal purposes.

The reality: Many people get married without registering — they perform a religious ceremony and consider themselves married. The marriage is valid in practice, but without a certificate, they face issues when: opening joint bank accounts, applying for visas, buying property, claiming spousal benefits, or if legal disputes arise.
Best practice: ALWAYS register your marriage even if you've had a religious ceremony. Registration is easy, costs ₹100-500, takes 2-3 weeks, and requires just 2 witnesses.
The certificate solves countless problems later.
Grace period for old marriages: If you got married 5, 10, or even 20 years ago and never registered, you can still register TODAY. There's no time limit.

You'll just need to provide: (1) Your marriage date and place, (2) Photos from your wedding or invitation card, (3) An affidavit on stamp paper stating the marriage date, (4) 2 witnesses (can be anyone — friends, relatives).

sections.heading: Two Routes to Marriage Certificate: Hindu Marriage Act vs. Special Marriage Act
rows: Any Indian citizen (any religion, inter-faith, inter-caste marriages)
30 days minimum (due to public notice period) + 3 days after notice
Public notice is posted — neighbors/community may find out
Same-religion Hindu, Sikh, Jain, Buddhist marriages
Inter-faith marriages (Hindu-Muslim, Hindu-Christian), inter-caste, love marriages, court marriages, civil unions
Divorce under Special Marriage Act (different provisions)
Longer due to notice period, can be controversial

sections.heading: How to Register Marriage Online — Step-by-Step

steps.title: Find your state's marriage registration portal
steps.description: Each state has its own portal. Common ones: Delhi (edistrict.delhigovt.nic.in), UP (igrsup.gov.in), Maharashtra (aaplesarkar.mahaonline.gov.in), Karnataka (kaveri.karnataka.gov.in), West Bengal (wbxpress.gov.in), Tamil Nadu (eservices.tn.gov.in). For your state, search '[Your State] marriage registration online' or visit the e-District portal. All are free and open 24/7 for application.

steps.title: Choose marriage act: Hindu Marriage Act or Special Marriage Act
steps.description: For same-religion Hindu/Sikh/Jain/Buddhist marriages: Select 'Hindu Marriage Act' (Form 19). For inter-faith, love marriages, or court marriages: Select 'Special Marriage Act' (Form 18). This choice depends on the nature of your marriage — if unsure, consult the portal or Sub-Registrar office. This choice determines which law governs your marriage and future divorce if needed.

steps.title: Fill the online application form
steps.description: Complete form with: Bride's details — name (as on Aadhaar), DOB (must be 18+), Aadhaar number, religion, caste (if applicable), educational qualification, address. Groom's details — same information (must be 21+ for groom). Marriage details — date of marriage (DD/MM/YYYY), place of marriage (city/village), type of ceremony (religious/court). Witness details — 2-3 witnesses (adults with valid IDs like Aadhaar, Voter ID, or Passport) — their names, relationship to couple (friend/family), and contact numbers.

steps.title: Upload required documents
steps.description: Digital uploads: (1) Aadhaar card (front and back) of both bride and groom. (2) Marriage photos — at least 1 clear joint photo of couple together (from wedding day or recent), or marriage invitation card with both names. (3) Affidavit of marriage on stamp paper (₹10-100 depending on state) — notarized statement by both bride and groom confirming the marriage date, place, and that they got married voluntarily. (4) Age proof — birth certificate, 10th marksheet, passport, or Aadhaar for both. (5) Address proof — Aadhaar, utility bill (electricity/water, max 3 months old), bank statement, lease agreement. (6) ID photo of both bride and groom (passport-size, clear face, no filters). (7) If previously married — divorce decree or death certificate of previous spouse.

steps.title: Pay fee and book appointment at Sub-Registrar office
steps.description: Application fee: ₹100-500 depending on state (Delhi ₹100, UP ₹300, Maharashtra ₹250, Karnataka ₹200 — varies). Payment methods: online (debit/credit card, net banking, UPI), or in person at the office. After payment, you get an application number. Book an appointment at your nearest Sub-Registrar office (or SDM office, depending on state) for physical verification. Appointment is usually within 7-15 days.

steps.title: Attend office appointment with both bride, groom, and 2-3 witnesses
steps.description: On the appointment date, BOTH bride and groom must be physically present with 2-3 witnesses (adult friends or family with valid IDs). The Sub-Registrar or SDM will: (1) Verify identities of bride, groom, and witnesses from submitted documents, (2) Ask questions to confirm marriage details, (3) Obtain signatures of bride, groom, and witnesses on the application form, (4) Take thumbprint of both bride and groom (for official record). The process takes 15-30 minutes. Witnesses need not sign — they just need to be present with ID. For Special Marriage Act, the 30-day public notice period starts AFTER this meeting.

steps.title: Marriage certificate issued
steps.description: For Hindu Marriage Act: Certificate is issued within 7 days of office verification. For Special Marriage Act: After 30-day public notice period, if no objections are raised, certificate is issued on day 31 (or 33 if counting business days). You get: (1) Digital certificate (PDF) downloadable from the portal, (2) Physical printed certificate sent by post or collected from the office. Both are equally valid. Total timeline: Hindu Marriage Act = 7-15 days. Special Marriage Act = 30-45 days.

sections.heading: Documents Needed for Marriage Registration
rows: Front and back, must have linked mobile number, used for OTP verification
Birth certificate, 10th marksheet, Passport, or Aadhaar
Aadhaar, utility bill (max 3 months old), bank statement, lease agreement, rental agreement
Joint photo from wedding or recent, or marriage invitation card with both names
Notarized (₹10-100), signed by both, stating marriage date, place, and voluntary consent
Aadhaar, Voter ID, Passport, or Driving License (adults with valid IDs)
Certified copy from court, required for second marriage
Certified copy required, for second marriage after spouse's death
30x40mm, white background, no glasses/headgear, clear face visible

sections.heading: Special Marriage Act: The 30-Day Public Notice Period
content: For Special Marriage Act registrations, a 30-day public notice is mandatory. Here's what happens: After you submit your application and meet the Sub-Registrar, your marriage details are posted publicly — a notice board at the Sub-Registrar office and sometimes in local newspapers, announcing that you intend to marry.
Any third party who objects (e.g., claiming you're already married to someone else, underage, or under duress) can file objection within 30 days.

Why the notice period: It's meant to prevent bigamy (marrying multiple people), forced marriages, and other frauds. After 30 days, if no objections are raised, your marriage is presumed valid and the certificate is issued.

Controversy about the notice period: For inter-faith and love marriages, this public notice has caused serious problems. Communities, families, or extremists sometimes use the notice period to intimidate couples, threaten divorce, or create social pressure.
Some couples abandon their marriages due to this. Some states like Bihar, Uttarakhand, and others have challenged the notice period and reduced it to 15 days or 7 days.
Check your state's current rules.

How to handle notice period issues: If you face harassment or threats due to the notice period, contact the local police immediately. The notice period cannot be waived, but you can seek police protection.
Some state governments have issued directions protecting couples from harassment. Apply for anticipatory bail or protective order from the court if needed.

sections.heading: Registering Old Marriages (No Time Limit)
content: If you got married 5, 10, 20, or even 50 years ago and never registered, you can register TODAY. There's NO time limit for marriage registration.

You'll just need to provide: (1) Actual date of marriage (DD/MM/YYYY), (2) Place of marriage (city/village/district), (3) Proof of marriage date — wedding photos, marriage invitation, newspaper announcement, or testimony from witnesses who were present. (4) Affidavit on stamp paper (₹10-100) signed by both bride and groom confirming the marriage date and place and that it was a voluntary marriage.

Process for old marriages: (1) Gather the above documents. (2) Visit your nearest Sub-Registrar office (under whose jurisdiction you currently live or where you got married). (3) File application for 'Belated Marriage Registration'.
(4) Provide all documents and pay fee (₹200-500). (5) Sub-Registrar verifies documents and may ask questions to confirm marriage details. (6) Certificate issued within 7-30 days.
Example: A couple married in 1995 in a temple, had children, lived together for 25 years, but never registered. They can still register in 2026.
The certificate will show the original marriage date (1995) — it's backdated to the actual marriage date, not the registration date.

Important: For old marriages, if one spouse has died, surviving spouse can register alone. The deceased's spouse can visit the Sub-Registrar office, provide proof of marriage (photos, witnesses), and get the certificate issued in both spouses' names with marriage date showing the actual date of marriage.

faqs.question: Is marriage registration mandatory in India? What happens if I don't register?
faqs.answer: Technically yes — the Supreme Court (2006) made it mandatory. However, enforcement varies. Unregistered marriages are legally valid (if performed under religious customs), but you'll face serious issues: Cannot open joint bank accounts, spouse visa applications rejected, insurance benefits won't transfer to spouse, inheritance rights become complicated, divorce takes longer, and government schemes may not recognize you as married. Best practice: ALWAYS register your marriage for legal security.

faqs.question: We got married 5 years ago and never registered. Can we still register?
faqs.answer: Yes, absolutely. There's no time limit for marriage registration. You can register 5, 10, 50 years after the wedding. Provide: (1) Actual marriage date, (2) Place of marriage, (3) Marriage proof (photos, invitation, witnesses), (4) Affidavit on stamp paper confirming marriage date. The certificate will show the original marriage date (backdated), not the registration date. Fee: ₹200-500. Processing: 7-30 days. This is called 'Belated Marriage Registration'.

faqs.question: Which Act should we use — Hindu Marriage Act or Special Marriage Act?
faqs.answer: Use Hindu Marriage Act if: Both are same religion (both Hindu, both Sikh, both Jain, both Buddhist). Use Special Marriage Act if: Inter-faith (Hindu-Muslim, Christian-Hindu), inter-caste, or court marriages. Both are legally valid. Hindu Marriage Act is faster (no 30-day notice), but Special Marriage Act provides equal rights. No conversion needed for Special Marriage Act — both retain their religions.

faqs.question: What is the 30-day notice period in Special Marriage Act? Can it be waived?
faqs.answer: It cannot be waived — it's mandatory by law. After you apply, your marriage details are posted publicly for 30 days. Any third party can object if they claim you're already married, underage, or under duress. If no objections, certificate is issued on day 31. The notice period has caused problems for inter-faith couples facing community pressure or harassment. If harassed, contact police. Some states reduced it to 15 days — check your state's rules. Processing timeline: ~45 days total (including notice period).

faqs.question: Is court marriage the same as Special Marriage Act registration?
faqs.answer: Court marriage is a civil ceremony performed by a judge/magistrate instead of a religious ceremony. Court marriages are automatically registered under the Special Marriage Act. So court marriage = marriage under Special Marriage Act. You still get a marriage certificate the same way (with 30-day notice period), but the ceremony happens in court instead of a temple/mosque/church.

faqs.question: One of us is NRI/foreigner. Can we register marriage in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. An NRI (Non-Resident Indian) or foreign national can marry an Indian citizen. Registration process is the same — apply at the Sub-Registrar office in India where the marriage took place. NRI/foreigner can apply under Special Marriage Act. Provide: Passport (for foreigner), Indian visa (if applicable), same documents as Indian citizen, and 2-3 witnesses. Certificate is issued in both names. Required for visa/passport updates, inheritance, and legal recognition in both countries.

faqs.question: How do I register marriage if one spouse is deceased?
faqs.answer: Surviving spouse can register the marriage alone. Provide: (1) Death certificate of deceased spouse, (2) Proof of marriage (photos, invitation, witnesses), (3) Affidavit on stamp paper by surviving spouse. The certificate will be issued in both spouses' names (showing marriage date, not death date). This is useful for: claiming inheritance rights, survivor pension, insurance benefits, or for children's succession. Processing: 10-20 days.

faqs.question: We want to marry but are in different religions. Which Act applies?
faqs.answer: Special Marriage Act. It explicitly allows inter-faith marriages — Hindu-Muslim, Hindu-Christian, Sikh-Christian, etc. No conversion is needed. Both retain your religions. The marriage is recognized equally by all religions and by the government. However, Special Marriage Act includes the 30-day public notice period (to prevent fraud), which can invite social pressure. Some states reduced this period. Check your state's current rules.

faqs.question: Do we need 2 or 3 witnesses for marriage registration?
faqs.answer: Hindu Marriage Act: 2 witnesses required. Special Marriage Act: 3 witnesses required. Witnesses can be anyone — friends, relatives, colleagues — as long as they're adults with valid ID (Aadhaar, Voter ID, Passport, Driving License). Witnesses don't sign the form, but they must be physically present during office verification. Some states relax this for online applications — check your state's portal.

faqs.question: How much does marriage registration cost?
faqs.answer: Fee varies by state: Delhi ₹100, UP ₹300, Maharashtra ₹250, Karnataka ₹200, West Bengal ₹150, Tamil Nadu ₹200. Additional cost: Affidavit on stamp paper (₹10-100 for drafting). Online application: Free. Physical certificate copies: Usually free with registration, but additional copies cost ₹10-50 each from Sub-Registrar. Total cost typically ₹100-500 depending on state.

disclaimer: Marriage registration processes, fees, and portal URLs vary significantly by state. This guide reflects general Indian procedures as of March 2026. Always verify with your state's Sub-Registrar office or official e-District portal for exact requirements and fees. State laws may change — consult local authorities for the most current information. For inter-faith marriages or complex situations, consult a marriage lawyer.
